article.title: Alibaba's XuanTie C950 Sets RISC-V Performance Record as China Mounts Coordinated Push for Chip Independence
article.summary: Alibaba's 5nm RISC-V processor scores over 70 on SPECint2006, while the Chinese Academy of Sciences unveils the Xiangshan open-source core, as Beijing accelerates its drive toward semiconductor self-sufficiency.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

In the span of a single week, two of China's most powerful institutions unveiled high-performance RISC-V processors that together signal the most significant escalation yet in Beijing's effort to build a computing stack independent of Western chip architectures. On March 24, Alibaba's DAMO Academy launched the XuanTie C950, a 5nm RISC-V CPU whose single-core score exceeds 70 on the SPECint2006 benchmark -- a new world record for the open instruction set. Two days later, the Chinese Academy of Sciences introduced the Xiangshan open-source RISC-V core and the openRuyi operating system at the 2026 Zhongguancun Forum in Beijing.

The back-to-back announcements underscore a coordinated national strategy: one commercial titan delivering server-grade silicon, and the country's premier research institution providing an open-source hardware-software stack available to the entire domestic industry.

## What We Know

### XuanTie C950: A New RISC-V Benchmark

The XuanTie C950, developed by Alibaba's T-Head semiconductor division, features an 8-wide superscalar out-of-order microarchitecture with a 16-stage pipeline and an out-of-order execution window exceeding 1,000 instructions, according to [CNX Software](https://www.cnx-software.com/2026/03/25/alibaba-xuantie-c950-a-powerful-rva2364-bit-risc-v-core-for-edge-ai-computing/). At its maximum clock speed of 3.2 GHz, the chip achieves a single-core SPECint2006 score above 70, which Alibaba claims is the highest ever recorded for a RISC-V processor.

The chip is compliant with the RVA23 RISC-V profile and supports all optional extensions, including RISC-V Vector Extension v1.0 with Vector Crypto and Alibaba's proprietary Attached Matrix Extension (AME) ISA, as [CNX Software](https://www.cnx-software.com/2026/03/25/alibaba-xuantie-c950-a-powerful-rva2364-bit-risc-v-core-for-edge-ai-computing/) detailed. Its configurable cache hierarchy offers up to 3 MB of private L2 cache per core and up to 8 MB of shared L3, with clusters scaling to eight cores via the XL-300 interconnect.

A key differentiator is the integrated Tensor Processing Engine (TPE), which delivers 8 TOPS per unit and supports data types from FP16 down to INT4 and FP8, as well as the newer MXFP8 and RVFP4 formats, according to [The Register](https://www.theregister.com/2026/03/25/alibaba_damo_xuantie_c950_chip/). Alibaba says the C950 can natively run large language models with hundreds of billions of parameters, including Qwen3-256B and DeepSeek V3-671B.

The chip has been verified on a 5nm process node. While Alibaba has not disclosed the manufacturing partner, [TrendForce](https://www.trendforce.com/news/2026/03/25/news-alibaba-unveils-risc-v-xuantie-c950-cpu-for-ai-agents-5nm-chip-reportedly-made-by-tsmc/) reported that sources indicate TSMC is the fabrication partner. T-Head's semiconductor division has shipped 470,000 chips as of February 2026 and reached annualized revenue surpassing RMB 10 billion, according to [TrendForce](https://www.trendforce.com/news/2026/03/25/news-alibaba-unveils-risc-v-xuantie-c950-cpu-for-ai-agents-5nm-chip-reportedly-made-by-tsmc/).

### Xiangshan: The Open-Source Alternative

At the Zhongguancun Forum on March 26, the Chinese Academy of Sciences introduced the Xiangshan processor alongside the openRuyi operating system, a native OS designed to run on RISC-V hardware, according to [China Daily](https://www.chinadaily.com.cn/a/202603/26/WS69c54702a310d6866eb4016e.html). Bao Yungang, deputy director of the CAS Institute of Computing Technology, described Xiangshan as "currently the most powerful open-source RISC-V processor in the world," citing a measured performance of 16.5 points per GHz on SPEC CPU2006.

The Xiangshan project has assembled more than 600 hardware researchers and 400 software researchers, with talent programs attracting over 27,000 participants from more than 1,100 universities globally, according to [China Daily](https://www.chinadaily.com.cn/a/202603/26/WS69c54702a310d6866eb4016e.html). Unlike Alibaba's proprietary C950, Xiangshan's entire design is open-source, making it freely available to Chinese chipmakers who lack the resources to develop high-performance cores independently.

### RISC-V's Growing Chinese Footprint

The dual announcements arrive as Chinese organizations have become the dominant force in RISC-V governance. Eleven of the 23 premier members of RISC-V International are Chinese organizations, including Alibaba Cloud and Huawei, according to the [South China Morning Post](https://www.scmp.com/tech/big-tech/article/3348168/chinas-tech-self-sufficiency-drive-reaches-new-milestone-powerful-risc-v-chips). The open nature of the RISC-V instruction set -- free of licensing fees and export restrictions -- has made it a strategic priority for Beijing as US export controls continue to restrict access to advanced x86 and Arm technologies.

## What We Don't Know

Despite the record-setting benchmarks, significant questions remain. [The Register](https://www.theregister.com/2026/03/25/alibaba_damo_xuantie_c950_chip/) noted that the C950's SPECint2006 performance "nearly matches Apple's M1 chip from 2020," suggesting a multi-year lag versus current Western designs. Neither Alibaba nor the CAS disclosed specific commercialization timelines or pricing for products based on these cores.

The software ecosystem remains the primary bottleneck for broader RISC-V adoption. Industry experts cited by the [South China Morning Post](https://www.scmp.com/tech/big-tech/article/3348168/chinas-tech-self-sufficiency-drive-reaches-new-milestone-powerful-risc-v-chips) identified software maturity as the key challenge, even as hardware performance narrows the gap with established architectures. It is also unclear whether the reported TSMC fabrication arrangement for the C950 could be affected by future tightening of US export controls on advanced node manufacturing.

Alibaba CEO Yongming Wu acknowledged the gap directly, stating that "Chinese chips lag behind those from western chipmakers," and positioning the co-design of chips with Alibaba's cloud infrastructure and Qwen AI models as the competitive differentiator rather than raw performance, according to [The Register](https://www.theregister.com/2026/03/25/alibaba_damo_xuantie_c950_chip/).

## Analysis

The strategic significance of these announcements lies not in any single chip but in the two-track approach they reveal. Alibaba's C950 targets immediate commercial deployment in its cloud data centers, optimized for AI inference workloads. The CAS Xiangshan project, by contrast, serves as a shared national resource -- an open-source foundation that any Chinese company can adopt, modify, and manufacture.

This dual strategy addresses one of the central vulnerabilities exposed by US semiconductor export controls: reliance on a small number of proprietary architectures controlled by foreign companies. By investing in both a proprietary high-performance path and an open-source ecosystem path, China is building redundancy into its semiconductor independence plan.

The integration of AI acceleration directly into the CPU -- rather than relying on discrete GPUs -- also reflects a broader industry trend toward heterogeneous computing, where inference workloads are distributed across purpose-built processing units embedded alongside general-purpose cores. Whether RISC-V can close the remaining performance gap with Arm and x86 in data center workloads will depend as much on software toolchain maturity as on silicon design.
